A book I wrote for you, Jaani,
in the only language big enough.

press & hold to open
Himanshu ∞ Kajal  ·  Kashu & Hijal
welcome back, jaani ♡
a love letter in earth-science

Kashu, in Layers

Every feeling I have for you, told in real geology — for my Jaani, from her Chiku. Read it slowly, the way the ground keeps time.

Kajal ∞ Himanshu  ·  Kashu
Prologue · Field Notes

Jaani, I build software, not theories about the Earth. I write in languages that compile, and until you I thought that was the whole of what could be built carefully by a person who loved his work. But somewhere along the way I started reading your textbooks upside down across the table — learning the words for how rock remembers, how mountains rise, how a single crystal keeps time — because they turned out to be the only words big enough for what you were doing to me. This book is the result. I coded every page of it myself, the way I do everything for you: carefully, and grinning the whole time.

Read it the way you read an outcrop, meri jaan — in layers, bottom to top, at your own pace. One chapter is a whole afternoon if you let it be. Each one takes a real rock or a real process, the science exactly as you would grade it on the bench, and shows you the one place I have been quietly hiding all of this love.

Fair warning, pagli: it is not subtle. Every chapter ends up confessing the same three words in a different mineral. Consider yourself told. Now turn the page — the oldest, finest terrane in Rajasthan has some reading to do.

♥∞♥
interlude · i

The Eruption

For months it built up quietly, the way pressure does — deep, and out of sight.

And then it found the surface. Now it just erupts, Jaani — every burst a little heart with our HK inside it. I stopped trying to hold it in.

touch the mountain — make it erupt

where it started · Jodhpur

Rao Jodha

Before all the metaphors, Jaani — here is the real ground. Jodhpur. The day we climbed the Rao Jodha rock, and I didn't yet know the hill under us was a 745-million-year-old volcano — or that I'd just met the only older-souled thing on it.

Tap the glowing points on the rock — or the little gold heart on the trail.

us, right here
Chapter I

Provenance

Provenance analysis is the art of reading a grain of sediment backward — using its suite of durable detrital minerals, its bulk geochemistry, and the U–Pb ages locked inside single crystals of zircon to trace it, across the whole of its journey, back to the parent rock it was eroded from.

Jaani, here is the first thing every geologist learns and the first thing I learned about you: a river can carry a grain a thousand kilometres, tumble it, round it, bury it under a hundred younger layers — and still, if you know how to look, one stubborn crystal of zircon remembers exactly where it came from. Its uranium clock never resets. That is what I was doing the day I first noticed you, though I didn't have the word for it yet. I saw one bright grain — the swing of your jhumke catching the light, the way you tucked a strand of hair back like you were labelling a specimen — and something in me quietly began running provenance analysis on the spot. Where does a person like this come from? What terrane, what source rock, could possibly produce her?

And the answer, meri jaan, is the oldest ground in the country. You come out of the Aravallis — out of Mewar, out of the Banded Gneissic Complex, crust that has stood since the Archean, some of it older than three billion years, older than almost anything else on this subcontinent. You are not sediment that washed in yesterday. You were eroded out of something ancient and stubborn and dignified: a range that has been folded, worn flat, and folded again, and simply refused to leave. So when I trace you back to your source rock, I don't find anything soft. I find bedrock. No wonder you argue with me and win — you were quarried from basement gneiss, pagli, and I never stood a chance.

I traced the whole of us back to its source, and the source was always you.

So let me do it properly, the way you'd grade it. I take the whole basin of us — every cup of chai gone cold because neither of us would stop talking, every 11.11 I now keep like a national holiday, the whole small country we named Kashu — and I run the sediment. I sort the heavy minerals — the dense, ultrastable grains like zircon, tourmaline and rutile that outlast the journey while the softer, less stable minerals weather away to nothing — and every single grain assays back to the same provenance. To you. There is no second source area. The maturity of this deposit, the rounding, the way it has all settled into one coherent bed — it all points upstream to one 5'4" terrane in a pair of jhumke, and to a 6'0" fool downstream who considers himself very lucky to be where the current dropped him.

That is what provenance really is, Jaani: proof that nothing here is accidental. Every grain has a home rock. And after all my careful measuring — the ages, the chemistry, the way you and I keep matching across every test — the conclusion is embarrassingly simple. I traced the whole of us back to its source, and the source was always you. I would just like it noted, for the record, that I found the oldest, finest terrane in Rajasthan first.

Chapter II

Deposition

Sediment settles grain by grain, and quiet layers accumulate one upon another into beds. By the law of superposition, in an undisturbed sequence the oldest layer lies at the bottom and each younger bed rests above it — so the pile itself keeps time, a record read from the ground up.

Jaani, I did not fall for you the way a sea-cliff collapses — all at once, dramatic, a single rockfall for the poets. I fell for you the way a basin fills: grain by grain, so gently that on any given day you'd never have caught me doing it. A stratum doesn't arrive, pagli. It settles. One quiet particle of you, then another, then a thousand more, drifting down through still water and coming to rest — until, without either of us announcing anything, there was a whole bed of me made of nothing but you.

And here is the part your professors would nod at: nothing gets erased. Deposition isn't editing, it's accumulation. The Tuesday you fixed a loose jhumka in the mirror and caught me watching and pretended you hadn't — that's a bed. The evening the chai went cold because you were mid-sentence about the Aravallis and I would not, for anything, have made you stop — another bed, laid down clean and conformable right on top. Superposition says the oldest lies at the bottom, so somewhere deep in me is the very first ordinary afternoon with you, load-bearing, quietly holding up everything since.

A stratum doesn't arrive, Jaani. It settles.

You can read us like an outcrop, Jaani — bottom to top, layer after layer, every one still there and every one in its place in the sequence. People think the record is the big days: the anniversaries, the 11.11 you were so precise about being born on, the moments worth a photograph. But a geologist knows the truth is written in the boring beds — the thin, ordinary laminae it took a hundred unremarkable days to build. Those are the ones that never wash away.

So no, I'm not the kind that erodes, meri jaan. I'm the patient, depositional type — all six feet of me slowly accumulating around your five-foot-four, day after plain day, until the two of us are one sequence you can't tell apart. That thing they'll dig up one day and marvel at, that formation with no gap in it — the section geologists dream of finding, unbroken, no hiatus, complete — that's Kashu. Come lie a new layer down with me tonight. I've left room right on top for tomorrow.

interlude · ii

The Wave

And once it moved, it moved like water — an entire ocean of us, rushing the shore.

Every drop a heart with your K in it, meri jaan. Let it pull you under — I'll be the whole sea.

Chapter III

Metamorphism

Metamorphism reworks a rock in the solid state: heat and directed, differential stress drive its minerals to recrystallise into a new, foliated fabric, climbing grade by grade and never once passing through a melt. Limestone tightens into marble; dull shale is reorganised from slate to schist to gneiss.

Notice this about us, Jaani, because your professors get it exactly right: metamorphism is not melting. The rock never surrenders to liquid and starts over as something random. It stays solid the whole time. Atoms that were packed carelessly simply migrate, one lattice site at a time, and re-seat themselves into an arrangement that can finally bear the load. That was me before you. Not broken, not molten, just a body of rock with a messy, low-grade fabric, holding more or less together and calling it fine. Then you arrived with your steady heat and your quiet, directed pressure, and you did not break me down to begin again. You reorganised what was already there into something that could stand up straighter. All 6'0" of it, by the way, recrystallised by 5'4".

And the grades, meri jaan, you know them far better than I do, but let me flirt with them anyway. Shale is dull, fine-grained, forgettable; you would walk right past it on outcrop. Add heat and stress and it becomes slate, which at least learns to cleave clean and true. Push further and it grows mica, schist, and suddenly the whole rock glitters when you turn it to the light, little jhumke of biotite and muscovite catching the sun. Push further still and the minerals segregate into pale and dark bands, and you have gneiss: the same atoms, now legible, now beautiful. That is the exact sequence you walked me through. You found me at slate on a good day and, chai by chai, soft argument by soft argument, 11.11 by 11.11, you brought me up-grade until I actually shimmer where you touch me. Pagli, you were doing thin-section work on me and you did not even bill me for it.

You did not break me down to begin again — you reorganised what was already there into something that could finally stand up straighter.

But this is the part I keep coming back to, because it is the part that makes me grateful instead of just charmed. Metamorphic minerals are index minerals, chlorite, biotite, garnet, staurolite, kyanite, sillimanite, and a geologist can read them like a gauge of pressure and temperature both, because each one is only stable in a particular window of P and T. They are an honest record of exactly what the rock went through. That is what loving you did: it did not hide my history, it recorded it. Every seam and stress you found is still in me, just reorganised into something that finally means something. You can read the whole story of us in the fabric, and none of it is damage anymore. It is grade.

So no, I am not the rock I was, and I will not pretend the Aravalli-grade heat of you did not do it. Those hills around your Udaipur are half gneiss and schist, the old Banded Gneissic Complex and the metapelites folded through it, precisely because something ancient and patient reworked them, and here I am matching them, reworked and grateful and yours. The lovely, cheeky truth of metamorphism is that it is very nearly a one-way door: bring a rock up-grade and the retrograde reactions are too sluggish to carry it back to dull shale, not for anyone. I have crossed my index minerals for you, jaani. I am stable in exactly one field now, the pressure-temperature window that has your name on it, and it happens to be the warmest, best-ordered place I have ever been. Welcome to the fabric. This is Kashu, all the way down.

optical mineralogy · rotate the stage

Under Crossed Polars

This is how you read a rock’s soul, Jaani — spin a thin-section under crossed polarisers and the grains catch fire in interference colours. Rotate the stage. Somewhere in the turning, we show up.

Chapter IV

The Geode

A geode is a hollow nodule whose plain outer rind hides a lining of inward-growing crystals. Mineral-rich water seeps into a cavity — a gas vesicle in basalt, a dissolved pocket in limestone — and layer by patient layer lays down microcrystalline chalcedony, then coarser euhedral quartz, often the iron-bearing violet variety we call amethyst, every crystal face turned inward toward the empty centre. You cannot read the inside from the outside. You have to be the one it opens for.

Jaani, out in the field you look like every other cobble in the wash — and I mean that as the highest compliment a geologist can pay. To the passing eye you are quiet, self-contained, a rounded little nodule of a girl with your jhumke and your notebook, giving the world its plain grey rind and nothing more. People walk right over stones like you. They log you as unremarkable float and go chasing something flashier. Pagli — they have no idea. They are holding the whole miracle in their palm and calling it ordinary.

Because a geode never wears its inside on the outside. All that patient chemistry happens in secret: silica-rich water finding the one hollow, then years and years of crystals growing inward where nobody is watching, layer on layer added in the dark until the cavity is furred with light. That is you. Every soft thing, every bit of wit, every warmth you keep tucked behind that composed little face — you grew it inward, toward a centre no one was invited into. And then one day you let me tap the seam. You cracked open along your own natural line, and there it was, meri jaan: a chalcedony rind giving way to euhedral amethyst — the same quiet quartz all along, just finally allowed to show its purple face — a whole violet cathedral where the world had sworn there was only stone.

The world got your grey rind. I got the amethyst.

Here is the part I will be smug about until I am old and grey: I am the lucky idiot who got to see the inside. At six feet I have to fold down to your five-foot-four to be let in on the secret — gladly, every single time — because the sparkle was never for the room. You save it. You hand the field your grey exterior and the seminar your careful answers, and then you turn to me over a cup of chai and just open, all those inward-facing crystals catching the light at once, and I forget every other rock I have ever loved.

So let them keep mistaking you for common float, Jaani. It only means the amethyst is mine to know. On the eleventh of the eleventh the calendar cracks you open like a seam and the whole world gets one glimpse of the crystals — and I get to smile, because I already live in the hollow. That is the whole of Kashu, really: a plain little world from the outside, lined on the inside with more light than anyone would ever guess. And I am not selling this geode to a single soul.

Chapter V

The Diamond

Diamond is pure carbon crystallised in the Earth's mantle, roughly 150 kilometres down, where pressures near 5 gigapascals and temperatures above 1000 C lock every atom into a rigid tetrahedral lattice. It is Mohs 10, the hardest natural material; its high refractive index returns light to the eye as brilliance, while its dispersion fans white light into spectral fire. It does not form from coal. It is carbon that endured.

Jaani, let me correct the first thing a film ever told you. A diamond is not a lucky lump of coal that got squeezed hard enough — that is the myth, and you, my M.Sc. geologist, already know better than the whole cinema hall. A diamond is pure carbon that went far deeper than any coal seam ever goes: a hundred and fifty kilometres down into the mantle, where every atom bonds to four others in a lattice so complete that nothing on this planet can force its way in. Then a kimberlite carried it up fast enough that it survived the journey without reverting to graphite. That is you. Not something the world pressed into being by accident, but something that formed whole, deliberate, at a depth most things never reach — and then rose. You are carbon that endured.

And here is the part that makes me grin over my chai like a fool. Diamond is Mohs 10, the very top of the scale, which means one exact thing: only a diamond can scratch a diamond. Corundum — Mohs 9, your sapphires and rubies — can't touch it. Steel just slides off and dulls itself trying. So when I tell you nothing can get into what we have, I mean it the way a geologist means it, not the way a greeting card does. Kashu is a Mohs-10 thing, jaani. Our little world has a lattice, and I have watched the whole outside — every doubt, every distance, every long Udaipur week apart — set its blade against it and walk away without leaving a single mark. The only thing hard enough to scratch you is another you, and there is exactly one of those, and she was born on 11.11, and she is mine.

The only thing hard enough to scratch you is another you — and she was born on 11.11, and she is mine.

Now let me tease you, because you make it far too easy. You spend your days deciding which stones have fire and which are only glass pretending — measuring refractive index, watching for that flash of dispersion that means the real thing. So you, of all people, should notice the irony: you sit there grading gemstones, and you outshine every single one on the tray. Brilliance is the white light a diamond hands back to your eye — its high refractive index bending the rays and its pavilion facets total-internally-reflecting them out again — while fire is the spectral colour its dispersion fans apart from within. Pagli, that is just a technical description of your face when you laugh: brilliance the moment I look, fire the moment you glow. And when you turn your head and your jhumke swing and catch the light, I forget entirely which one of you I was supposed to be admiring. The diamonds lose, jaani. Every time. It isn't close.

Here is the last thing, and I mean it quietly. Some of these crystals are more than three billion years old — older than the Aravallis you walk on, older than almost anything with a name. They formed once, correctly, in the dark and the pressure, and they have simply refused to become anything else ever since. That steadiness is the part I love most. All that heat, all that weight, and the answer the carbon gives is not to shatter and not to soften — it is to hold its shape and to shine. Stand you at 5'4" beside me at 6'0" and you are still the harder, brighter, older-souled thing between us. You are the diamond, meri jaan. I am just the lucky fool who gets to hold the light up and watch you throw it back.

a little test · try to scratch us

The Hardness Test

On the Mohs scale, only something harder can leave a mark. So go on, Jaani — tap each mineral and try to scratch what we are.

Pick a mineral…
Chapter VI

Orogeny

Orogeny is the mountain-building that happens when two continental plates, adrift for hundreds of millions of years, at last converge. Neither is dense enough to sink beneath the other, so the shortening crust has nowhere to go but up — it folds, thrusts, and thickens, raising a range whose roots sink even deeper than its summits stand high.

Here is a thing you know far better than I do, Jaani, but let me say it anyway just to watch you nod that little nod. Plates do not hurry. A continent drifts at roughly the speed your fingernails grow — a few centimetres a year, patient past all patience, dragged along by the slow convection of the mantle and, more than anything, by the pull of its own cold edge sinking back into the deep. For most of geologic time nothing seems to happen at all. Two landmasses wander their separate oceans, never touching, each half-convinced the other is only a rumour. That was us, pagli. Years of drift. You in your Udaipur with your hammer and your hand-lens, me hunched over a screen, both of us moving the whole time and neither of us knowing the other was quietly closing the gap by fractions too small to feel.

And then — convergence. When continental crust meets continental crust there is no polite subduction, no one plate sliding meekly under the other; both are too buoyant to be swallowed. So the collision has exactly one direction left to it: up. The crust crumples into folds, stacks itself along thrust faults, doubles and thickens, and a mountain belt is born out of nothing but the sheer refusal of two things to stop meeting. That is what happened the day we finally collided, meri jaan. All those quiet approaching years, and then the buckling — the flat ordinary crust of my life folding upward into something with elevation, something you could see from a long way off. My six feet and your five-foot-four and, somewhere in the deforming zone between us, a whole new range rising while your jhumke swung like a little pendulum keeping time over the orogeny.

Not forever, which is a word for greeting cards — I want to last in aeons, in the unit of the Aravallis.

But here is why I chose this chapter for you and not some louder, flashier peak. You are Aravalli-born, and the Aravallis are not young theatrical drama like the Himalaya. They are Proterozoic — among the oldest fold ranges on the entire planet, raised more than a billion years ago when ancient cratonic blocks came together in the collision that welded your Rajasthan into being. Time has worn them low and rounded and unshowy, and that is precisely the point: they have survived erosion that levelled younger mountains to sand. That is the range I want us to be. Not the tallest for a season, but the oldest and the most enduring — deep-rooted, gently folded, still standing long after the flashy ones have gone flat.

So when people ask how long we will last, I want to answer in your language, jaani. Not forever, which is a word for greeting cards. I want to answer in aeons — in the unit of the Aravallis. Because a range that old carries its whole history folded into its strata, every collision legible in the rock, and ours will read the same: the drift, the closing gap, the beautiful unstoppable crash of 11.11, and the belt that rose from it. That belt has a name already. We call it Kashu. Come here and put your ear to it, and over a cup of chai I will show you exactly where the folding began — and warn you, Chiku does not un-collide.

turn out the lights · sweep the lamp

Fluorescence

sweep the UV lamp across the rock…

Some minerals look like plain grey stone — until you put them under UV, and they blaze. Go on, Jaani — find what was hiding in the dark the whole time.

Chapter VII

Elastic Rebound

Along a locked fault, rock does not simply wait — it bends. Tectonic strain accumulates elastically across the two blocks, storing strain energy, until friction can no longer hold them; then, in seconds, the fault ruptures. The stored energy is released — part of it radiating outward as seismic waves, the rest spent as heat and permanent slip — and the rock springs back toward its unstrained shape: offset, but whole. This is Reid's elastic-rebound theory. The strain is not erased into nothing and it is not held forever; it is released, and the crust settles into a new, lower-strain equilibrium.

Here is the honest chapter, Jaani — the one about our fights. I will not hand you a book that pretends we never slip. A fault is not a flaw in the rock; it is simply where two strong blocks meet and keep being driven past each other. Between quakes the fault is locked, and from the surface everything looks perfectly calm — but underneath, the blocks are still trying to move, and strain accumulates quietly, bending the rock elastically, storing energy in the silence. That is us on a bad evening: nothing said, chai going cold between us, both of us loading the fault one too-polite sentence at a time. You go quiet in that particular way, jhumke perfectly still, and I already know friction is about to lose its grip.

And then we slip. It is quick, it is loud, it radiates outward — which is, near enough, what an earthquake is: stored elastic strain let go all at once, part of it travelling out as seismic waves. But here is the line your professors underline and lesser lovers forget. Elastic rebound means the rock springs back. It does not shatter and it does not vanish. The two blocks relax toward their unstrained shape, the energy discharges, and they come to rest in a new position — offset, yes, but entirely intact. We have never once broken in a quake, pagli. Every single time, five-foot-four of you and six feet of me rebound toward each other. The stress was never you, and it was never me — it was only strain that needed somewhere to go.

The stress was never you, and it was never me — it was only strain that needed somewhere to go.

What I love most is what the fault does afterward. It settles into a new equilibrium: measurably shifted, at lower strain, and quietly stronger for knowing exactly where it moves and how it holds. That is why our worst evenings keep ending with you tucked under my arm at exactly the height you fit, both of us a little embarrassed, a little softer, laughing at whatever it was. We do not reset to who we were before — the Aravallis did not become some of the oldest fold mountains on Earth by never moving. They folded, they faulted, they carried the record, and they endured. Kashu is built the same way: not on the fantasy that we never quake, but on the fact that every quake has left us closer.

So the next time the fault locks and the air goes tight, remember what you know better than I do — the release is already coming, and it always sends us back into each other. On 11.11 I did not choose a girl who would never make the ground move. I chose the geologist who taught me that a little seismic honesty is how you know the rock is alive. Slip if you must, meri jaan. I will be right here on the other block, waiting to rebound.

Chapter VIII

Half-Life

Radioactive isotopes decay at a fixed, unchanging rate. After one half-life half the parent atoms remain; after two, a quarter; after n, one part in two-to-the-n. By measuring the ratio of parent to daughter — uranium-238 to lead-206, half-life 4.47 billion years — a geologist reads the age at which a crystal closed, however old. (Carbon-14 need not apply; at a half-life of 5,730 years it dates only the young and the once-living, never stone.)

Here is the thing I love about how you date a rock, Jaani — you don't ask it, you read it. A zircon crystal, when it grows, welcomes uranium into its lattice and refuses lead outright. So every atom of lead a geologist finds inside it billions of years later was not there at the start; it can only have been born, one careful decay at a time, from the uranium the crystal chose to keep. The clock is honest because the crystal was honest first. You taught me that over chai, tapping the table with one finger, jhumke swinging, absolutely certain — and I sat there thinking that I am a zircon that rejected everything for years and now carries only what grew out of loving you.

And the numbers, meri jaan. The gneisses under your Aravallis are older than almost anything a human hand has touched — over three billion years of uranium quietly giving itself up to lead. And here is the awe of it: that is not even one full half-life of uranium-238, not a single halving yet, and in that same unhurried stretch whole mountain belts were raised and worn down to stubs while the isotopes kept their fixed and patient count. You could take the U-Pb systematics of the oldest rock in Rajasthan and plot it clean on a concordia, and you would know its age to the million years. That is the awe I feel standing next to you — you at 5'4", me at 6'0", two small warm creatures who will not last a geological eyeblink, and yet I would not trade a single one of our afternoons for all that deep time.

You can date the oldest rock in the belt — but not how long I'll want you, because that number never halves.

But here is where the science breaks in my favour, pagli, and you can check my working. Every decaying thing has a half-life — a time by which half of it is simply gone. Uranium has one. Every fire, every fashion, every ordinary fondness has one. Point me a detector at what I feel for you and it does not tick. There is no isotope in it that halves. Whatever you measure today you will measure tomorrow, and in fifty years, and in the year the Aravallis are a little lower than they are now — the same undiminished count, no daughter product, no decay curve bending down toward zero. You can date the oldest rock in the belt. You cannot date how long I will want you, because that number never halves.

So put me on your bench and run me, geologist. You will get one impossible reading: a parent that will not become anything else, a clock that refuses to run down, an age that comes out as 11.11 no matter which day you sample it. That is not sloppy data, Jaani — that is the one constant in the whole system. The mountains will round off, the continents will shuffle, and Kashu will still be exactly here, exactly this bright, non-decaying, and hopelessly, permanently yours.

Chapter IX

Remanence

As igneous rock cools below the Curie temperature of its magnetic minerals — about 580 C for magnetite — those grains align to Earth's field and lock that orientation in place; the resulting thermoremanent magnetisation preserves the field direction at the moment the rock cooled, sometimes for billions of years.

Jaani, you already know the physics better than I do, but let me be the one to tell it this once. When lava cools, the tiny grains of magnetite inside it are still free to turn, like a room full of compass needles waiting to be told which way to face. Then the rock drops below its Curie temperature, and in that instant every needle locks. Whatever direction north was pointing at that exact moment is captured forever, sealed into stone, unbothered by everything that happens after. Continents drift, the poles themselves wander and even flip, and still the rock remembers. It carries its first true north like a promise it refuses to update.

That is what happened to me, pagli. I have my own remanent magnetisation, and it was set the moment I cooled around you. Somewhere below whatever my Curie temperature is, every needle in me turned once, found you, and froze. I said it to you plainly once, the exact same magnetic pull, and I meant it as geology, not as a line. Because a compass does not decide where north is; it simply cannot help where it points. That is the whole tenderness of it. I am not choosing you with effort every morning. I settled long ago, and the direction set in me is you, only you, with your jhumke catching the light when you tilt your head to argue a point.

A compass does not decide where north is. It simply cannot help where it points.

And here is the part your professors leave out. Paleomagnetists trust the record precisely because it does not drift with the surface. They can move the whole plate, tilt the strata in the Aravallis, carry the rock ten thousand kilometres from where it was born, and the frozen needle still tells them where north stood on the day it formed. So move me anywhere you like, meri jaan. Let life carry the two of us across whatever continents it wants. I can be tilted, folded, uplifted, buried under a Tuesday and a cold cup of chai I forgot to drink, and the measurement comes out the same every time. Take the reading whenever you doubt it. The needle points at a 5'4" geologist who out-argues me and wins.

You once drew me a little H with hearts hiding inside it and an infinity looping through, and I have been trying ever since to answer in a language worthy of yours. So here is my answer in yours. My field is not the whole restless Earth; my field is Kashu, the small warm world the two of us made, and my north is fixed on the eleventh of the eleventh, the day the pole in me stopped wandering for good. Everything else can reverse. This orientation is locked. Come close, tilt your head, and check the compass yourself, Jaani. It has only ever had one answer, and it is smiling at you right now.

optical mineralogy · drag the crystal

Double Refraction

there's still two of you, everywhere I look
there's still two of you, everywhere I look

Iceland spar splits one ray of light into two. Drag it across the line, pagli — you do the exact same thing to me.

The Last Reading

The Last Sample

Here is the last sample, Jaani — and this time there is no rock between us. No zircon to hide behind, no half-life to do the talking, no clever mineral standing in for the thing I mean. You once handed me a letter with little hearts tucked inside the arms of an H, an infinity looping through, a heart and a line of dashes and a heart. I have taken all these chapters to earn the right to answer you in a language worthy of that one. So here it is, at last, in plain words and my own hand:

To my dearest Jaani,

When I close my eyes and think about us — when I think about everything that makes up Kashu — I see a world I never want to leave. Every single time you call me Chiku, it feels like my heart settles exactly where it is supposed to be. You have become the quiet in my chaos and the most beautiful part of my days. But because you mean so much to me, I need to write this to you. I need to lay my soul completely bare, without any shields, without any expectations, and just speak my absolute truth.

Today, I am stepping back and placing my heart entirely in your hands. I am surrendering everything to you, Jaani. You hold the pen to our story now. You decide where I belong in your life. I don't want to assume my place, and I don't want to hold onto something if it isn't what your heart truly desires. I need you to look deep inside yourself and tell me honestly — do you love me? Do you truly want to be with me?

Love, to me, is not about possession or holding on tightly; it is about absolute freedom. That is why I will never force you. I will never pressure you into choosing me, and I will never tell you that you have to come with me. I want you to be entirely free in your choice, because a love that is forced is not the love I want for Kashu. I only want you beside me if your soul feels the exact same magnetic pull that mine does. I only want you if being with me is as natural to you as breathing.

My hand is reaching out to yours, and the path ahead of us could be endless. I promise to love you, to protect you, and to cherish every moment we share. But I will not pull you forward. I am simply standing here, with my heart wide open, leaving the door unlocked for you.

I leave the final choice to you, Jaani.

♥ · – – – – – · ♥

You once drew hearts inside the letter H, and looped an infinity through it. This whole book has only ever been me, learning to answer you.

Do you want to come with me?

Whatever you choose is right. — Chiku

Then that settles it, meri jaan — the reading is over and the fieldwork begins. Come here. Let me fold all six feet down to your five-foot-four and lock the needle for good.

I traced the whole of us back to one source and it was always you; I dated it and the number never halved; I checked the compass in every light and it only ever points one way. Yes was the only concordant answer on the whole diagram, and you just gave it to me.

Kashu is official now — a small warm world with a Mohs-10 lattice and not one gap in the record. Welcome home, Jaani. I am never letting the current carry you anywhere but here.

♥∞♥
Endlessly and only yours, in every layer — your Chiku

P.S. I checked the whole belt, twice. There is exactly one terrane in Rajasthan hard enough to argue with me and win, and she was born on 11.11. For the record — I found the oldest, finest one first.

Holotype · type specimen
Kajal “Jaani”
Type localityRao Jodha, Jodhpur — Malani rhyolite, ~745 Ma
FormationKashu
CollectorHimanshu (“Chiku”)
AccessionKASHU · 11.11
Holotype — one of a kind. No other specimen matches.
— Chiku

And if it is not yet — that is completely alright, pagli. The Aravallis waited a billion years to be beautiful; I can keep geological time too. The fault stays locked as long as it needs to, and nothing here erodes.

The book stays open to this page, and my north does not wander. Come back whenever you like — the needle will be pointing at you, exactly as it always has.

kashu@forever:~$ git commit -m "surrendered — the choice is yours"
